_schema_documentation.purpose: Complete research methodology taxonomy for CoThesis medical research training platform

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Single most important differentiating feature

vs_similar_methods.difference: How they differ

methodologies.name: Retrospective Chart Review
methodologies.description: A retrospective observational study design that systematically extracts and analyzes data from existing medical records to answer a specific research question. The researcher reviews patient charts, electronic health records, or medical databases to collect information about past events, treatments, and outcomes. This is the most common research methodology undertaken by medical trainees due to its accessibility, relatively low cost, and ability to be completed within training timeframes.

included_subtypes.name: Single-site chart review
included_subtypes.description: Data collected from one hospital or clinic

included_subtypes.name: EMR data extraction
included_subtypes.description: Automated extraction from electronic health records

included_subtypes.name: Hybrid chart review
included_subtypes.description: Combination of EMR extraction and manual review

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Data already exists in medical records - no prospective data collection required

vs_similar_methods.difference: Chart review is data collection METHOD; cohort is analytic DESIGN comparing exposed vs unexposed
Audit compares practice against standards; chart review may be descriptive or hypothesis-testing
Case series describes patients with condition; chart review broader scope

methodologies.name: Cross-Sectional Survey
methodologies.description: A study design that collects data from a population at a single point in time (or brief period) to examine the prevalence of exposures and/or outcomes. Participants complete questionnaires or surveys, and associations can be examined between variables, but temporal relationships cannot be established. Extremely common for trainees - 43.6% of US internal medicine residents conducted survey research. Often uses validated instruments or develops new questionnaires.

included_subtypes.name: Knowledge/attitudes/practice (KAP) survey
included_subtypes.description: Measures what people know, believe, and do

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Single time point data collection via questionnaire - snapshot of population

vs_similar_methods.difference: Cross-sectional is single time point; longitudinal follows over time
Survey emphasizes questionnaire data; analytic may use clinical measures
Survey is snapshot; cohort follows outcomes over time

methodologies.name: Retrospective Cohort Study
methodologies.description: An observational study that identifies a cohort of individuals from historical records, classifies them by exposure status at a past time point, then follows their records forward to determine outcomes. Both exposure and outcome have already occurred when the study begins. Uses existing data sources (medical records, registries, administrative databases). Common for trainees - 31.9% of US internal medicine residents conducted retrospective cohort studies. More rigorous than simple chart review as it employs formal cohort methodology.

included_subtypes.name: Registry-based retrospective cohort
included_subtypes.description: Uses disease or procedure registry data

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Follows cohort from exposure to outcome using historical data - classic epidemiological design applied retrospectively

vs_similar_methods.difference: Retrospective uses historical data; prospective follows forward in real-time
Cohort requires formal exposed/unexposed comparison; chart review may be descriptive
Cohort starts with exposure; case-control starts with outcome

methodologies.name: Prospective Cohort Study
methodologies.description: The gold standard observational design where a cohort is identified, baseline exposures are measured, and then participants are followed forward in real-time to observe outcome occurrence. Data is collected as events happen, allowing for accurate measurement of exposures and temporal relationships. Less common for trainees due to time requirements but highly valued when completed. Requires significant planning and infrastructure.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Real-time forward follow-up from exposure measurement to outcome occurrence

vs_similar_methods.difference: Prospective collects data in real-time; retrospective uses historical records
Cohort observes natural exposures; RCT assigns interventions
Cohort measures exposures/outcomes; survey may focus on attitudes/behaviors

methodologies.name: Case-Control Study
methodologies.description: An observational design that starts by identifying individuals with an outcome (cases) and without (controls), then looks backward to compare exposure histories. Efficient for studying rare diseases or outcomes because cases are selected based on outcome status. Calculates odds ratios rather than relative risks. Requires careful control selection. Moderately feasible for trainees when case identification is straightforward.

included_subtypes.name: Hospital-based case-control
included_subtypes.description: Cases and controls from same hospital

included_subtypes.name: Density-sampled case-control
included_subtypes.description: Controls sampled at case occurrence time

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Starts with outcome (cases vs controls) and looks backward to exposure

vs_similar_methods.difference: Case-control starts with outcome; cohort starts with exposure
Standard case-control is standalone; nested is within existing cohort
Case-control has comparison group; case series is descriptive only

methodologies.name: Nested Case-Control Study
methodologies.description: A case-control study conducted within an existing cohort. Cases are cohort members who develop the outcome; controls are sampled from cohort members at risk when each case occurs (incidence density sampling). Combines efficiency of case-control design with temporal clarity of cohort. Often used for biomarker studies where stored samples can be analyzed only for cases and matched controls rather than entire cohort.

included_subtypes.name: Registry-nested case-control
included_subtypes.when_to_use: Large registry with detailed baseline data

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Case-control design embedded within pre-existing cohort using incidence density sampling

vs_similar_methods.difference: Nested is within defined cohort; standard may use any control population
Nested samples new controls per case; case-cohort uses single subcohort
Nested analyzes subset; full cohort analyzes everyone

methodologies.name: Case-Cohort Study
methodologies.description: A design within an existing cohort where all cases are compared to a randomly selected subcohort drawn at baseline. Unlike nested case-control (which samples new controls for each case), case-cohort uses the same subcohort for all analyses. Efficient when studying multiple outcomes from expensive exposure measurements. The subcohort represents the source population throughout follow-up.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Single subcohort sampled at baseline serves as comparison for all cases across multiple outcomes

vs_similar_methods.difference: Case-cohort uses single subcohort; nested samples controls per case
Case-cohort analyzes subcohort plus cases only; full cohort analyzes everyone
Case-cohort within defined cohort; standard may lack cohort structure

methodologies.name: Ecological Study
methodologies.description: A study that uses aggregate data at the population or group level rather than individual-level data. Compares disease rates between populations (countries, regions, time periods) with aggregate exposure measures. Useful for generating hypotheses and examining population-level associations. Subject to ecological fallacy - associations at group level may not apply to individuals. Relatively feasible for trainees using publicly available data.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Uses aggregate population-level data rather than individual-level measurements

vs_similar_methods.difference: Ecological uses group data; cohort tracks individuals
Ecological compares populations; cross-sectional surveys individuals
Ecological may be descriptive; time series uses statistical modeling

recommended_sources: Journal of Epidemiology and Community Health

methodologies.name: Longitudinal Panel Study
methodologies.description: A prospective design that follows the same individuals over time with repeated measurements at multiple time points. Enables analysis of within-person change and individual trajectories. Differs from simple cohort studies by emphasizing repeated measures on the same variables. Common in social science and health services research. Challenging for trainees due to extended timeframe required.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Repeated measurements on same individuals over time enabling within-person change analysis

vs_similar_methods.difference: Panel emphasizes repeated measures; cohort may have single outcome assessment
Panel follows same people; repeated cross-sectional surveys different samples
Panel has individual-level repeated measures; time series typically aggregate

methodologies.name: Registry Analysis
methodologies.description: Research using data from clinical registries - systematic collections of health information about patients with specific conditions, procedures, or exposures. Registries like SEER (cancer), NSQIP (surgical quality), and disease-specific registries provide large, standardized datasets. Popular for trainee research because data already exists with established quality controls. Enables population-level questions with real-world data.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Uses standardized clinical registry data with established collection protocols and quality measures

vs_similar_methods.difference: Registry is clinical purpose-built; administrative is billing/claims
Registry has standardized variables; chart review extracts from unstructured records
Registry is data source; cohort is study design that can use registry data

methodologies.name: Administrative Database Study
methodologies.description: Research using large administrative databases created for billing, enrollment, or operational purposes rather than research. Includes claims databases, hospital discharge data, and health system administrative records. Provides very large sample sizes and population coverage but limited clinical detail. Variables reflect what is captured for administrative purposes. Common for health services research and pharmacoepidemiology.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Uses data collected for billing/administrative purposes - very large but limited clinical depth

vs_similar_methods.difference: Administrative is billing-derived; registry is clinically purpose-built
Administrative is claims/billing; EMR is clinical documentation
Administrative is coded structured data; chart review extracts from records

methodologies.name: Secondary Data Analysis
methodologies.description: Research that re-analyzes existing datasets collected for previous studies or ongoing surveillance programs. Uses publicly available datasets like NHANES, BRFSS, or data from completed trials/studies. Cost-effective way to answer new research questions without primary data collection. Requires understanding of original study design and data limitations. Excellent option for trainees due to accessibility and lower cost.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Re-analyzes existing research datasets to answer new questions not addressed in original study

vs_similar_methods.difference: Secondary uses existing data; primary collects new data
Secondary analyzes raw data; meta-analysis synthesizes results
Secondary uses research datasets; registry uses clinical registries

methodologies.name: Cross-Sectional Analytic Study
methodologies.description: A cross-sectional design that goes beyond description to test associations between exposures and outcomes measured at the same time point. Calculates prevalence ratios or prevalence odds ratios. Cannot establish temporal sequence (which came first). More analytically rigorous than descriptive cross-sectional but shares limitations regarding causation. Common transitional design for trainees learning analytical epidemiology.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Tests exposure-outcome associations at single time point with appropriate analytical methods

vs_similar_methods.difference: Analytic tests associations; descriptive reports prevalence only
Cross-sectional measures both simultaneously; case-control samples on outcome
Cross-sectional is single time point; cohort follows over time

methodologies.name: Prevalence Study
methodologies.description: A descriptive study designed specifically to estimate the proportion of a population with a disease, condition, or characteristic at a specific point in time (point prevalence) or during a defined period (period prevalence). Focuses on accurate prevalence estimation rather than testing associations. Important for understanding disease burden and healthcare planning. Straightforward for trainees when population and case definitions are clear.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Primary goal is accurate estimation of disease/condition frequency in defined population

vs_similar_methods.difference: Prevalence focuses on estimation; analytic tests associations
Prevalence is existing cases; incidence is new cases
Prevalence estimates burden; screening identifies cases for intervention

recommended_sources: Journal of Epidemiology and Community Health

methodologies.name: Incidence Study
methodologies.description: A study that measures the occurrence of new cases of a disease or condition in a population over a specified time period. Calculates incidence rate (person-time denominator) or cumulative incidence (risk). Requires follow-up of disease-free population. More complex than prevalence studies because of need to identify new cases and track person-time at risk. Often embedded within prospective cohort designs.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Measures rate of new disease occurrence over time in at-risk population

vs_similar_methods.difference: Incidence is new cases; prevalence is existing cases
Incidence study may be purely descriptive; cohort tests exposure-outcome
Incidence study is time-limited research; surveillance is ongoing monitoring

methodologies.name: Parallel RCT (Standard)
methodologies.description: The classic randomized controlled trial design where participants are randomly assigned to one of two or more intervention groups (including control) and followed in parallel. Each participant receives only one intervention. The gold standard for establishing causation in clinical research. Trainees typically contribute as co-investigators rather than leading due to complexity and resources required. CONSORT is the mandatory reporting standard.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Random allocation to parallel groups with each participant receiving only one intervention

vs_similar_methods.difference: Parallel has one treatment per person; crossover has each person receive multiple treatments
RCT randomizes allocation; cohort observes natural exposure
RCT uses true randomization; quasi uses non-random allocation

methodologies.name: Cluster RCT
methodologies.description: An RCT where randomization occurs at the cluster level (hospitals, practices, schools, communities) rather than individual level. All individuals within a cluster receive the same intervention. Used when individual randomization is impractical or would cause contamination. Requires special statistical methods accounting for clustering. Common in primary care, public health, and implementation research.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Randomization at group/cluster level rather than individual level

vs_similar_methods.difference: Cluster randomizes groups; individual randomizes people
Cluster is parallel; stepped-wedge has sequential crossover
Cluster RCT randomizes clusters; quasi assigns non-randomly

methodologies.name: Crossover RCT
methodologies.description: An RCT where each participant serves as their own control by receiving all treatments in sequence, with the order determined by randomization. A washout period between treatments allows the first treatment effect to dissipate. Efficient design requiring fewer participants because within-person comparisons reduce variability. Only suitable for conditions that are stable and treatments with reversible effects.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Each participant receives all treatments in randomized sequence - within-person comparison

vs_similar_methods.difference: Crossover has same person get all treatments; parallel has different people
Crossover pools data across participants; N-of-1 is for individual patient
Crossover randomizes treatment order; before-after has fixed sequence

methodologies.name: Stepped-Wedge RCT
methodologies.description: A cluster trial design where all clusters start in control condition, then sequentially cross over to the intervention at randomly assigned time points until all clusters receive the intervention. Useful when intervention is expected to be beneficial and withholding indefinitely is unethical, or when logistical constraints prevent simultaneous rollout. Popular in implementation research and health services.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Sequential cluster crossover from control to intervention at randomized time points

vs_similar_methods.difference: Stepped-wedge has all clusters eventually get intervention; parallel is fixed arms
Stepped-wedge has randomized timing; before-after is pre-planned
Stepped-wedge has randomization; time-series is observational

methodologies.name: Factorial RCT
methodologies.description: An RCT that simultaneously evaluates two or more interventions by randomizing participants to all possible combinations. A 2x2 factorial tests two treatments: A alone, B alone, both A and B, and neither. Efficient design that can test multiple hypotheses and detect interactions, but requires larger sample sizes for interaction testing. Assumes no interaction between treatments for main effects analysis.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Tests multiple interventions simultaneously by randomizing to all combinations

vs_similar_methods.difference: Factorial tests combinations; multi-arm tests separate interventions
Factorial has groups receive combinations; crossover has sequential treatments
Factorial is fixed; adaptive can drop/add arms

methodologies.name: Adaptive/Platform Trial
methodologies.description: A trial design that allows pre-planned modifications based on accumulating data while preserving validity. Can include response-adaptive randomization, sample size re-estimation, dropping ineffective arms, or adding new treatments. Platform trials are perpetual adaptive trials testing multiple treatments against common control. RECOVERY trial for COVID-19 is a famous example. Requires sophisticated statistical infrastructure.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Pre-planned data-driven modifications while trial is ongoing

vs_similar_methods.difference: Adaptive allows modifications; fixed design is unchangeable
Adaptive has multiple adaptation types; sequential focuses on stopping rules
Adaptive can modify; factorial tests fixed combinations

methodologies.name: Pragmatic Trial
methodologies.description: An RCT designed to test interventions in real-world conditions rather than ideal research settings. Uses broad eligibility, usual care comparators, flexible intervention delivery, and patient-relevant outcomes. Focuses on effectiveness (does it work in practice?) rather than efficacy (can it work under ideal conditions?). The PRECIS-2 wheel helps assess trial pragmatism across nine domains.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Designed for real-world applicability with broad eligibility and usual care conditions

vs_similar_methods.difference: Pragmatic tests effectiveness; explanatory tests efficacy
Pragmatic still randomizes; observational does not
Pragmatic tests effectiveness; Phase IV monitors safety

methodologies.name: N-of-1 Trial
methodologies.description: A randomized crossover trial conducted in a single patient, with multiple periods of active treatment and control (or alternative treatment). Designed to determine optimal treatment for an individual patient when population data is insufficient. The patient serves as their own control across multiple randomized periods. MOST FEASIBLE experimental design for trainees - can be conducted with single patient and provides high-quality individual evidence.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Randomized crossover trial in single patient to determine individual treatment response

vs_similar_methods.difference: N-of-1 is single patient; crossover pools multiple patients
N-of-1 has randomization; case report is observational
N-of-1 uses randomization; single-case may not

methodologies.name: Non-Inferiority Trial
methodologies.description: An RCT designed to show that a new treatment is not unacceptably worse than an established treatment, within a pre-specified margin. Used when new treatment has other advantages (safety, cost, convenience) that justify acceptance if efficacy is similar. Requires careful definition of non-inferiority margin based on clinical judgment and historical data. Statistical analysis differs from superiority trials.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Tests whether new treatment is not worse than standard by more than pre-specified margin

vs_similar_methods.difference: Non-inferiority accepts 'not worse'; superiority requires 'better'
Non-inferiority is one-sided; equivalence requires both directions
Non-inferiority has specific margin; comparative may not

methodologies.name: Equivalence Trial
methodologies.description: An RCT designed to demonstrate that two treatments have similar efficacy within a pre-specified equivalence margin in both directions. Tests that the difference between treatments lies within the margin (neither better nor worse). More stringent than non-inferiority because must rule out both directions. Common in bioequivalence studies for generic drugs.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Tests that treatments are similar in both directions within pre-specified margin

vs_similar_methods.difference: Equivalence is two-sided; non-inferiority is one-sided
Equivalence wants similarity; superiority wants difference
Equivalence has formal margin; comparative may be exploratory

methodologies.name: Pilot/Feasibility RCT
methodologies.description: A small-scale study conducted prior to a main RCT to test whether study procedures are feasible and acceptable. Pilot studies test intervention delivery and trial procedures. Feasibility studies assess whether a trial can be done. Not powered for efficacy outcomes - focuses on process outcomes like recruitment, retention, adherence, and acceptability. MORE FEASIBLE for trainees as lead investigators.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Tests trial procedures and feasibility rather than intervention efficacy

vs_similar_methods.difference: Pilot tests feasibility; definitive tests efficacy
Pilot focuses on process; Phase II on dosing/preliminary efficacy
Pilot tests trial procedures; proof-of-concept tests intervention mechanism

methodologies.name: Wait-List Control Trial
methodologies.description: An RCT where the control group receives the intervention after a delay rather than never receiving it. Ethically preferred when intervention is expected beneficial and withholding permanently is problematic. Control group provides comparison during wait period, then receives intervention. Common in psychological and behavioral interventions. Can demonstrate effects but control period is limited.

included_subtypes.name: Standard wait-list
included_subtypes.description: Control receives intervention after fixed delay

included_subtypes.name: Preference wait-list
included_subtypes.description: Choice to wait or receive immediately

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Control group receives intervention after delay - everyone eventually treated

vs_similar_methods.difference: Wait-list controls eventually get treatment; parallel may never
Wait-list is individual; stepped-wedge is cluster
Wait-list is one direction only; crossover goes both ways

methodologies.name: Preference-Based Trial
methodologies.description: A trial design that incorporates patient treatment preferences. Patients who have no preference are randomized; those with strong preferences receive their preferred treatment. Allows analysis of preference effects and comparison of randomized vs preference arms. Addresses concern that randomization may not reflect real-world treatment selection. Wennberg design and comprehensive cohort design are variants.

included_subtypes.name: Wennberg design
included_subtypes.description: Two-stage: willing to randomize, then randomize

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Incorporates patient treatment preferences into trial design structure

vs_similar_methods.difference: Preference design accommodates choice; standard randomizes regardless
Preference trial has randomized component; observational does not
Preference formally structures choice; pragmatic may not

methodologies.name: Zelen Design
methodologies.description: A controversial RCT design where randomization occurs before consent is obtained. Participants are randomized first, then approached for consent to receive the allocated treatment. Those who decline receive standard care. Proposed by Marvin Zelen to reduce consent bias and improve recruitment. Rarely used due to ethical concerns, but may be considered when consent process itself affects outcomes. Requires careful ethical justification.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Randomization occurs before rather than after obtaining informed consent

vs_similar_methods.difference: Zelen randomizes before consent; standard consents before randomization
Zelen is individual pre-consent; cluster randomizes groups
Zelen has specific consent design; registry uses existing infrastructure

methodologies.name: Phase 0 (Microdosing) Trial
methodologies.description: The earliest human studies using sub-therapeutic microdoses (typically 1/100th of expected pharmacological dose) to generate preliminary pharmacokinetic data. Helps determine if drug behaves in humans as predicted from animal studies. Very small participant numbers (10-15). Minimal risk due to microdoses. Not designed to assess efficacy or safety. Exploratory IND studies in FDA terminology. Rarely involves trainees.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Sub-therapeutic microdoses to assess early human PK before full Phase I

vs_similar_methods.difference: Phase 0 uses microdoses; Phase I uses pharmacological doses
Phase 0 is in humans; preclinical is in animals/lab
Phase 0 is pre-Phase I; first-in-human usually Phase I

methodologies.name: Phase I (Safety/Dosing) Trial
methodologies.description: First substantial human testing of a new drug or intervention, primarily to evaluate safety, tolerability, pharmacokinetics, and pharmacodynamics. Usually conducted in healthy volunteers (oncology excepted). Dose escalation to identify maximum tolerated dose (MTD) or optimal biological dose. Small sample sizes (20-100). Trainees may participate as sub-investigators in academic medical centers.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: First pharmacological-dose human testing focusing on safety and dosing

vs_similar_methods.difference: Phase I uses therapeutic doses; Phase 0 uses microdoses
Phase I focuses on safety/PK; Phase II focuses on efficacy
Phase I is humans; preclinical is animals

methodologies.name: Phase Ia (Single Ascending Dose)
methodologies.description: The first part of Phase I where single doses are administered to small cohorts with escalation to the next dose level after safety review. Typically 3-6 subjects per dose level. Evaluates acute safety and single-dose pharmacokinetics. Usually includes placebo controls within each cohort. Sentinel dosing (one subject first) often used for safety.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Single-dose administration with sequential dose escalation across cohorts

vs_similar_methods.difference: SAD is single dose; MAD is multiple doses
SAD is component of Phase I; full Phase I includes SAD+MAD
SAD is dose escalation phase; first-in-human is overall designation

methodologies.name: Phase Ib (Multiple Ascending Dose)
methodologies.description: The second part of Phase I where multiple doses are administered over days to weeks to evaluate steady-state pharmacokinetics, accumulation, and safety with repeated dosing. Follows SAD to establish dosing range. Typically 10-14 days of dosing per cohort. Important for understanding time-dependent changes in PK and identifying delayed toxicities.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Multiple-dose administration to assess steady-state PK and repeated-dose safety

vs_similar_methods.difference: MAD is multiple doses; SAD is single doses
MAD focuses on PK/safety; Phase II focuses on efficacy
MAD is short-term; chronic is long-term exposure

methodologies.name: Phase II (Efficacy) Trial
methodologies.description: Studies designed to evaluate preliminary efficacy of a treatment in patients with the target condition, while continuing to assess safety. First trials in patient populations. Larger than Phase I (100-300 participants). May be single-arm or randomized. Identifies optimal dose for Phase III. Trainees may participate as site coordinators or sub-investigators.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: First efficacy evaluation in target patient population with dose optimization

vs_similar_methods.difference: Phase II tests efficacy in patients; Phase I tests safety in healthy volunteers
Phase II is exploratory efficacy; Phase III is confirmatory
Phase II is drug development; pilot is procedure testing

methodologies.name: Phase IIa (Dose-Finding)
methodologies.description: The early part of Phase II focused on identifying the optimal therapeutic dose(s) to carry forward. Tests multiple doses to establish dose-response relationship. May use adaptive designs to efficiently identify best doses. Biomarker endpoints often used as surrogates. Relatively small samples per dose arm (20-50 patients each).

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Identifies optimal therapeutic dose through dose-response evaluation in patients

vs_similar_methods.difference: Phase IIa optimizes efficacy dose; Phase I finds MTD
Phase IIa selects dose; Phase IIb confirms efficacy
Phase IIa is regulatory designation; dose-finding is design type

methodologies.name: Phase IIb (Efficacy)
methodologies.description: The later part of Phase II that provides more robust efficacy data at the selected dose(s). Often randomized and controlled. Larger than Phase IIa (100-300 patients). Provides go/no-go decision for Phase III investment. Also called 'pivotal Phase II' when designed to potentially support approval (especially rare diseases, breakthrough therapy).

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: More robust efficacy evaluation at optimized dose to support Phase III decision

vs_similar_methods.difference: Phase IIb confirms efficacy; Phase IIa finds dose
Phase IIb is exploratory; Phase III is confirmatory
Phase IIb may be pivotal in rare disease; usually Phase III is pivotal

methodologies.name: Phase III (Comparative/Confirmatory) Trial
methodologies.description: Large-scale confirmatory trials designed to demonstrate efficacy and safety for regulatory approval. Randomized, often double-blind, comparing to standard of care or placebo. 300-3,000+ participants across multiple sites. Primary evidence for drug approval. Trainees commonly participate as sub-investigators at academic sites. Most visible clinical trial phase.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Large-scale confirmatory trial to establish efficacy/safety for regulatory approval

vs_similar_methods.difference: Phase III is confirmatory; Phase II is exploratory
Phase III is pre-approval; Phase IV is post-marketing
Phase III is registration-focused; pragmatic is real-world effectiveness

methodologies.name: Phase IV (Post-Marketing) Trial
methodologies.description: Studies conducted after regulatory approval to monitor real-world safety, effectiveness, and optimal use. Includes post-marketing surveillance, comparative effectiveness research, and additional indication studies. Large populations (1,000-10,000+). May be required by regulators (REMS in US). Detects rare adverse events not seen in Phase III. Ongoing throughout drug lifecycle.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Post-approval studies monitoring real-world use, safety, and effectiveness

vs_similar_methods.difference: Phase IV is post-approval; Phase III is pre-approval
Phase IV is structured study; pharmacovigilance is spontaneous reporting
Phase IV is any post-marketing; pragmatic is real-world effectiveness design

methodologies.name: First-in-Human Study
methodologies.description: The very first administration of a new drug or biologic to humans. Represents transition from preclinical to clinical development. Highest-risk phase requiring extensive preclinical data, careful dose selection, real-time safety monitoring, and specialized facilities. Usually Phase I design but designation emphasizes the milestone of first human exposure. Requires extensive regulatory preparation.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Very first human exposure to a new therapeutic agent

vs_similar_methods.difference: FIH is milestone designation; Phase I is regulatory phase
FIH uses pharmacological doses; Phase 0 uses microdoses
FIH is humans; preclinical is animals/in vitro

methodologies.name: Narrative Systematic Review
methodologies.description: A systematic review that uses narrative synthesis rather than statistical meta-analysis to summarize findings across studies. Follows systematic search methodology but synthesizes results through textual description, grouping, and tabulation. Appropriate when studies are too heterogeneous for pooling or when the research question is broad. HIGHEST feasibility systematic review for trainees. 49% of RANZCP scholarly projects are literature reviews with 93% pass rate.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Systematic search methodology with narrative rather than statistical synthesis

vs_similar_methods.difference: Narrative uses textual synthesis; meta-analysis uses statistical pooling
Systematic review answers specific question; scoping maps breadth of evidence
Systematic review has explicit methods; narrative review may not be systematic

methodologies.name: Scoping Review
methodologies.description: A type of evidence synthesis that aims to map the key concepts, types of evidence, and gaps in a research area rather than answer a specific question. Uses systematic search methods but broader inclusion criteria than systematic reviews. Ideal for emerging fields or complex topics. Does not require quality appraisal. Experienced 4,200% growth in medical education literature. HIGHLY FEASIBLE for trainees.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Maps breadth of evidence on a topic rather than answering specific focused question

vs_similar_methods.difference: Scoping maps breadth; systematic answers specific question
Scoping uses systematic methods; narrative may be unsystematic
Scoping is comprehensive mapping; rapid accelerates for urgency

methodologies.name: Rapid Review
methodologies.description: A form of evidence synthesis that uses streamlined systematic review methods to produce evidence summaries quickly, typically for urgent policy or clinical decisions. May limit databases searched, date ranges, or use single reviewers. Trades some rigor for speed. Growing importance for rapid guidance development. Feasible for trainees with time constraints.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Systematic review methods adapted for accelerated timeline with specified shortcuts

vs_similar_methods.difference: Rapid uses shortcuts; systematic review is comprehensive
Rapid answers specific question quickly; scoping maps breadth
Rapid uses systematic methods; narrative may not

recommended_sources: Agency for Healthcare Research and Quality (AHRQ)

methodologies.name: Cochrane Systematic Review
methodologies.description: A systematic review conducted according to Cochrane Collaboration standards and published in the Cochrane Library. Represents gold standard methodology with rigorous protocols, peer review, and regular updates. Requires Cochrane Review Group affiliation. Takes 1-2+ years with extensive support. Very high prestige but rarely feasible for trainees to lead; contributing as co-author more realistic.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Systematic review meeting Cochrane Collaboration's gold-standard methodology and peer review

vs_similar_methods.difference: Cochrane has specific methodology, peer review, updates; standard may vary
Cochrane uses GRADE; JBI has own methods
Cochrane has full editorial process; PROSPERO is registration only

methodologies.name: Living Systematic Review
methodologies.description: A systematic review that is continuously updated as new evidence becomes available, rather than being a static snapshot. New studies are incorporated on regular schedule (e.g., monthly). Requires infrastructure for ongoing surveillance and updates. Valuable for rapidly evolving topics like COVID-19. Gaining prominence but resource-intensive. Not feasible for typical trainee projects.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Continuously updated systematic review rather than static point-in-time snapshot

vs_similar_methods.difference: Living is continuously updated; standard is static
Living has ongoing surveillance; update is periodic revision
Living is comprehensive ongoing; rapid is quick one-time

methodologies.name: Umbrella Review
methodologies.description: A systematic review of systematic reviews - synthesizing evidence from multiple existing systematic reviews on the same topic. Provides highest-level evidence synthesis. Useful when many systematic reviews exist with varying conclusions. Assesses quality of component reviews. Sometimes called 'overview of reviews'. Moderately feasible for trainees when existing reviews are available.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Systematic review of existing systematic reviews - highest level of evidence synthesis

vs_similar_methods.difference: Umbrella reviews reviews; systematic reviews primary studies
Umbrella synthesizes reviews; NMA compares interventions indirectly
Umbrella critically synthesizes reviews; scoping maps evidence

methodologies.name: Realist Review
methodologies.description: A theory-driven systematic review approach that aims to understand how complex interventions work, for whom, and in what circumstances. Focuses on identifying Context-Mechanism-Outcome (CMO) configurations rather than simple effect sizes. Appropriate for complex social interventions where context matters significantly. More interpretive than aggregative. Requires theoretical sophistication.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Theory-driven review identifying Context-Mechanism-Outcome configurations

vs_similar_methods.difference: Realist builds theory; standard aggregates effects
Realist uses CMO framework; meta-ethnography uses reciprocal translation
Realist develops explanatory theory; scoping maps evidence

methodologies.name: Critical Interpretive Synthesis
methodologies.description: A systematic review approach that combines systematic searching with interpretive analysis to generate new theory or conceptual frameworks from a body of literature. Bridges systematic review rigor with qualitative interpretive analysis. Appropriate for generating theory from complex literature. Produces 'synthetic constructs' and integrating arguments rather than aggregated findings.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Systematic review methods combined with interpretive analysis for theory generation

vs_similar_methods.difference: CIS generates theory; systematic review aggregates findings
CIS can include any literature type; meta-ethnography typically qualitative
CIS generates new theory; realist tests/refines programme theories

methodologies.name: Integrative Review
methodologies.description: A review method that allows inclusion of diverse research methodologies (experimental and non-experimental) to provide a comprehensive understanding of a phenomenon. Combines data from theoretical and empirical literature. Common in nursing research. Uses systematic approach but broader than traditional systematic reviews in what can be included.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Includes diverse study types including theoretical and empirical literature for comprehensive synthesis

vs_similar_methods.difference: Integrative includes theoretical literature; systematic typically empirical only
Integrative may include theory; mixed methods focuses on qual+quant data
Integrative uses systematic methods; narrative may not

methodologies.name: State-of-the-Art Review
methodologies.description: A review that provides a comprehensive overview of the current state of knowledge on a topic, including recent advances, controversies, and future directions. Often commissioned or invited. Typically authored by subject matter experts. Less rigid methodology than systematic review but aims for comprehensive coverage. Common format for high-impact journal review articles.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Comprehensive expert-authored overview of current knowledge state without strict systematic methodology

vs_similar_methods.difference: State-of-the-art is expert-driven; systematic has rigid methodology
State-of-the-art aims for comprehensiveness; narrative may be selective
State-of-the-art is expert synthesis; scoping maps evidence systematically

methodologies.name: Standard Meta-Analysis
methodologies.description: Statistical synthesis that combines quantitative results from multiple studies to produce a pooled effect estimate. Uses inverse-variance weighting or similar methods. Produces forest plots showing individual and combined effects. Assesses heterogeneity across studies. The quantitative component of systematic reviews when studies are sufficiently similar. More complex than narrative synthesis but provides precise effect estimates.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Statistical pooling of effect sizes from multiple studies to produce combined estimate

vs_similar_methods.difference: Meta-analysis is statistical; narrative is textual
Standard MA is pairwise; NMA allows indirect comparisons
Standard uses published data; IPD uses raw individual data

methodologies.name: Network Meta-Analysis (NMA)
methodologies.description: A meta-analysis technique that allows comparison of multiple treatments simultaneously, even when direct head-to-head trials are lacking. Uses indirect evidence (A vs B and B vs C allows A vs C comparison) alongside direct evidence. Produces treatment rankings. Increasingly used for clinical guidelines. Requires specialized statistical methods. More complex than standard meta-analysis.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Combines direct and indirect evidence to compare multiple treatments simultaneously

vs_similar_methods.difference: NMA compares multiple treatments; standard is pairwise
NMA uses both direct and indirect; indirect uses only indirect
NMA provides statistical ranking; simple ranking is informal

methodologies.name: Individual Patient Data (IPD) Meta-Analysis
methodologies.description: A meta-analysis using raw, individual-level data from each included study rather than published aggregate results. Considered gold standard for meta-analysis. Enables consistent analysis across studies, subgroup analyses, and time-to-event analyses. Requires data sharing agreements with original investigators. Very resource-intensive. Rarely feasible for trainees to lead.

included_subtypes.name: Hybrid IPD meta-analysis
included_subtypes.description: Mix of IPD and aggregate data

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Uses raw individual-level data from each study rather than published summary statistics

vs_similar_methods.difference: IPD uses raw data; aggregate uses published summaries
IPD is systematic review of studies; pooled may be single dataset
IPD synthesizes existing trials; mega-trial is single large trial

methodologies.name: Cumulative Meta-Analysis
methodologies.description: A meta-analysis that sequentially adds studies in chronological order, showing how the pooled estimate evolves over time as evidence accumulates. Useful for demonstrating when sufficient evidence emerged, identifying unnecessary trials, or examining effect drift. Each row in the forest plot shows cumulative estimate including all studies up to that date.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Shows evolution of pooled estimate as studies are added sequentially over time

vs_similar_methods.difference: Cumulative shows temporal evolution; standard shows final pool
Cumulative is retrospective analysis; living is prospective updates
Cumulative adds by time; sensitivity varies assumptions

methodologies.name: Meta-Regression
methodologies.description: An extension of meta-analysis that uses regression techniques to examine the relationship between study-level characteristics (moderators) and effect sizes. Helps explain heterogeneity by identifying factors that influence treatment effects. Can examine continuous or categorical moderators. Requires sufficient number of studies for meaningful analysis (rule of thumb: 10 studies per covariate).

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Uses regression to examine how study characteristics moderate effect sizes

vs_similar_methods.difference: Meta-regression is statistical model; subgroup is stratified analysis
Meta-regression explains heterogeneity; standard reports it
Meta-regression uses study-level moderators; IPD can use individual-level

methodologies.name: Dose-Response Meta-Analysis
methodologies.description: A meta-analysis technique that examines the relationship between exposure dose/level and outcome across studies. Models the shape of the dose-response curve (linear, J-shaped, threshold, etc.). Common in nutritional epidemiology, pharmacology, and environmental health. Uses specialized methods for non-linear relationships and correlated dose levels within studies.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Models the shape of the relationship between exposure levels and outcomes

vs_similar_methods.difference: Dose-response models curve shape; standard pools single comparison
Dose-response models exposure levels; meta-regression uses study-level moderators
Dose-response synthesizes across studies; individual is within-study analysis

methodologies.name: Diagnostic Test Accuracy Meta-Analysis
methodologies.description: A meta-analysis of diagnostic test accuracy studies that pools sensitivity and specificity estimates. Uses bivariate or hierarchical models to account for the correlation between sensitivity and specificity. Produces summary ROC curves. Complex due to threshold effects and two correlated outcomes. Separate methods from intervention meta-analyses.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Pools diagnostic sensitivity and specificity with appropriate methods for their correlation

vs_similar_methods.difference: DTA pools sens/spec; intervention pools effect sizes
Meta-analysis synthesizes across studies; single study is one test evaluation
DTA MA pools across studies; ROC may be within-study analysis

methodologies.name: Prevalence Meta-Analysis
methodologies.description: A meta-analysis that pools prevalence estimates from multiple studies to produce a summary prevalence. Uses appropriate methods for proportions (Freeman-Tukey transformation or generalized linear mixed models). Common for disease burden estimation. Single proportion outcome rather than comparative effect size. Heterogeneity often high due to population differences.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Pools single-arm prevalence estimates rather than comparative effect sizes

vs_similar_methods.difference: Prevalence pools single proportions; intervention pools comparisons
Meta-analysis synthesizes multiple studies; single is one population
Prevalence MA is synthesis method; burden estimate may use various methods

methodologies.name: Prognostic Factor Meta-Analysis
methodologies.description: A meta-analysis examining the relationship between prognostic factors and patient outcomes across multiple studies. Pools hazard ratios or other prognostic effect measures. Challenging due to variable adjustment, reporting, and outcome definitions across studies. Important for identifying predictors of disease progression or treatment response.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Synthesizes evidence on factors that predict patient outcomes

vs_similar_methods.difference: Prognostic examines predictors; intervention examines treatments
Prognostic focuses on outcome prediction; risk factor on etiology
Prognostic examines single factors; prediction model reviews full models

methodologies.name: Bayesian Meta-Analysis
methodologies.description: A meta-analysis conducted within Bayesian statistical framework, incorporating prior knowledge and producing posterior probability distributions. Allows incorporation of external evidence, handles sparse data better, and provides probability statements about effects. Increasingly popular for complex meta-analyses including NMA. Requires Bayesian statistical expertise.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Uses Bayesian statistical framework with prior distributions and posterior probability statements

vs_similar_methods.difference: Bayesian uses priors and posteriors; frequentist uses p-values and CIs
Bayesian provides probability statements; standard provides significance tests
Bayesian is statistical approach; meta-regression is analytical technique

methodologies.name: Thematic Analysis
methodologies.description: The most accessible and widely used qualitative analysis method. Identifies, analyzes, and reports patterns (themes) within data. Theoretically flexible - can be used with various epistemological approaches. Braun & Clarke's 6-phase approach is most commonly cited. Excellent entry point for trainees new to qualitative research. Does not require deep theoretical commitment like grounded theory or phenomenology.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Flexible method for identifying patterns across qualitative data without specific theoretical requirements

vs_similar_methods.difference: Thematic is interpretive patterns; content analysis may be more quantitative
Thematic describes themes; grounded theory builds theory
Thematic is flexible; framework uses pre-defined framework

methodologies.name: Content Analysis
methodologies.description: A systematic method for analyzing text, documents, or media content by coding and categorizing material. Can be quantitative (counting frequencies) or qualitative (interpreting meaning). Often used for analyzing documents, media, or existing textual materials. More structured than thematic analysis. Common for analyzing policy documents, media coverage, or written records.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Systematic coding and categorization of textual/media content with possible quantification

vs_similar_methods.difference: Content analysis may quantify; thematic is interpretive
Content analysis categorizes content; discourse analyzes language use
Content analysis is specific method; document analysis is broader term

methodologies.name: Framework Analysis
methodologies.description: A matrix-based qualitative analysis method developed at the UK National Centre for Social Research. Uses a systematic five-stage process with data organized in a matrix framework. Particularly suited to applied policy research with specific questions and tight timelines. More transparent and structured than some qualitative approaches. Allows multiple researchers to work systematically.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Matrix-based systematic analysis with five-stage process for applied research

vs_similar_methods.difference: Framework uses matrix structure; thematic is more flexible
Framework is case-based matrix; content may be code-based
Framework uses specific matrix; template uses hierarchical coding

methodologies.name: Phenomenology (Descriptive)
methodologies.description: A philosophical and methodological approach focused on describing the essence of lived experience of a phenomenon. Descriptive phenomenology (Husserl tradition) aims to describe experiences as they appear, bracketing (epoché) researcher preconceptions. Seeks the essential structures of experience. More philosophically demanding than thematic analysis. Requires understanding of phenomenological philosophy.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Describes essential structures of lived experience while bracketing researcher assumptions

vs_similar_methods.difference: Descriptive describes experience; IPA interprets meaning-making
Phenomenology seeks essence; thematic identifies patterns
Phenomenology focuses on experience; narrative on story structure

methodologies.name: Interpretative Phenomenological Analysis (IPA)
methodologies.description: A qualitative approach combining phenomenological description with hermeneutic (interpretive) analysis. Focuses on how individuals make sense of their lived experiences. Involves double hermeneutic - researcher interpreting participants' interpretations. Typically uses small, homogeneous samples (3-6 participants). Developed by Jonathan Smith for psychology research. More structured than general phenomenology.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Interprets how participants make meaning of their lived experiences (double hermeneutic)

vs_similar_methods.difference: IPA interprets meaning-making; descriptive describes experience
IPA is idiographic first; thematic may be nomothetic
IPA focuses on sense-making; narrative on story structure

methodologies.name: Grounded Theory
methodologies.description: A systematic methodology for developing theory grounded in data through iterative collection and analysis. Uses theoretical sampling, constant comparison, and coding (open, axial, selective) to build theory from the ground up. Multiple schools exist (Glaser, Strauss & Corbin, Charmaz constructivist). More demanding than descriptive methods - requires theory generation not just description. Less feasible for trainees without mentorship.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Generates theory from data through iterative collection, comparison, and coding

vs_similar_methods.difference: Grounded theory generates theory; thematic describes patterns
Grounded theory builds theory; phenomenology describes experience
Grounded theory is iterative theory-building; content analysis categorizes

methodologies.name: Ethnography
methodologies.description: A methodology involving prolonged immersion in a cultural setting to understand social practices, beliefs, and behaviors from insider perspective. Originated in anthropology. Uses participant observation, field notes, and interviews. Requires extended fieldwork (months to years). Produces 'thick description' of cultural context. Challenging for trainees due to time requirements. Focused ethnography is more feasible adaptation.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Prolonged immersion in cultural setting with participant observation

vs_similar_methods.difference: Ethnography emphasizes culture; case study may use various methods
Ethnography studies culture; phenomenology studies experience
Ethnography describes culture; grounded theory generates theory

methodologies.name: Narrative Analysis
methodologies.description: A qualitative approach that focuses on stories people tell about their experiences. Analyzes the structure, content, and performance of narratives. Views stories as primary way humans make sense of experience. Multiple approaches exist (Labov structural, Riessman, Frank illness narratives). Particularly relevant for understanding illness experience, identity, and life transitions.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Analyzes stories as fundamental human sense-making, examining structure and meaning

vs_similar_methods.difference: Narrative preserves story form; thematic fragments into themes
Narrative focuses on story; IPA on meaning-making
Narrative analyzes stories; discourse analyzes language/power

methodologies.name: Discourse Analysis
methodologies.description: A family of approaches examining how language constructs social reality, power relations, and identities. Analyzes not just what is said but how language functions in social contexts. Multiple traditions exist (Foucauldian, critical, conversation analysis). Requires theoretical sophistication about language and power. Challenging for trainees without strong theoretical background.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Analyzes how language constructs reality, power, and identity in social contexts

vs_similar_methods.difference: Discourse examines language function; content categorizes content
Discourse analyzes language use; thematic identifies patterns
Discourse focuses on language/power; narrative on story structure

methodologies.name: Template Analysis
methodologies.description: A flexible qualitative analysis technique using hierarchical coding templates that can be developed a priori, from initial data, or combination. Associated with Nigel King. More structured than thematic analysis but more flexible than framework analysis. Uses hierarchical coding with higher-order and lower-order codes. Suitable for team-based analysis and large datasets.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Hierarchical coding template that develops iteratively through analysis

vs_similar_methods.difference: Template uses hierarchical codes; thematic uses themes
Template is more flexible; framework uses matrix structure
Template emphasizes hierarchy; codebook may be flat structure

methodologies.name: Meta-Synthesis
methodologies.description: An umbrella term for systematic approaches to synthesizing findings from multiple qualitative studies. Aims to produce new interpretations or higher-order themes beyond individual studies. Includes various specific methods (meta-ethnography, thematic synthesis, etc.). The qualitative equivalent of meta-analysis. Requires methodological expertise in qualitative research.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Systematic synthesis of qualitative studies to produce higher-order interpretations

vs_similar_methods.difference: Meta-synthesis is qualitative; meta-analysis is quantitative
Meta-synthesis specifically synthesizes qualitative studies
Meta-synthesis interprets; scoping maps evidence

methodologies.name: Meta-Ethnography
methodologies.description: A specific approach to qualitative synthesis developed by Noblit and Hare (1988). Uses translation of concepts across studies through reciprocal, refutational, and lines-of-argument synthesis. Most cited qualitative synthesis method. Produces third-order interpretations that go beyond original studies. Requires deep engagement with study findings and their relationships.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Translates concepts across qualitative studies using Noblit & Hare's seven-phase approach

vs_similar_methods.difference: Meta-ethnography uses translation; thematic uses theme synthesis
Meta-ethnography is interpretive; meta-aggregation is aggregative
Meta-ethnography translates; framework uses pre-existing framework

methodologies.name: Thematic Synthesis
methodologies.description: A method for synthesizing qualitative research developed by Thomas and Harden (2008). Uses three stages: coding text, developing descriptive themes, and generating analytical themes. More transparent and reproducible than meta-ethnography. Particularly suited for health services and intervention research. Accessible entry point to qualitative synthesis.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Three-stage synthesis generating analytical themes from descriptive themes

vs_similar_methods.difference: Thematic synthesis is more transparent; meta-ethnography uses translation
Thematic synthesis is for synthesis; thematic analysis for primary research
Thematic synthesis develops themes; framework uses existing framework

methodologies.name: Meta-Aggregation
methodologies.description: The Joanna Briggs Institute (JBI) approach to qualitative synthesis. Uses an aggregative rather than interpretive approach - assembling findings into categories and synthesized findings. Emphasizes pragmatic outputs for practice and policy. More structured and reproducible than interpretive methods. Produces evidence summaries and recommendations.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Aggregates qualitative findings into categories and synthesized statements for practice

vs_similar_methods.difference: Meta-aggregation aggregates; meta-ethnography interprets
Meta-aggregation is structured JBI method; thematic is more flexible
Meta-aggregation is synthesis method; CERQual assesses confidence

methodologies.name: Framework Synthesis
methodologies.description: A qualitative synthesis approach that uses an existing conceptual or theoretical framework to organize and synthesize findings from multiple studies. Can use a priori framework or 'best fit' framework approach where existing framework is tested and modified. Useful when clear theoretical perspective guides the synthesis. Particularly suited for policy-relevant reviews.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Uses existing theoretical framework to organize qualitative synthesis

vs_similar_methods.difference: Framework uses existing theory; thematic develops themes inductively
Framework uses pre-existing framework; meta-ethnography translates concepts
Framework synthesis is for synthesis; framework analysis for primary research

recommended_sources: Carroll et al best fit papers

methodologies.name: Convergent Parallel Mixed Methods
methodologies.description: A mixed methods design where quantitative and qualitative data are collected concurrently, analyzed separately, and then merged for comparison or integration. Equal priority typically given to both components. Aims for complementarity - understanding different aspects of the same phenomenon. Most common mixed methods design. Efficient but requires dual expertise.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Concurrent collection of quantitative and qualitative data with subsequent merging

vs_similar_methods.difference: Convergent is concurrent; explanatory is sequential quant→qual
Convergent is concurrent; exploratory is sequential qual→quant
Convergent merges after; embedded has one within other

methodologies.name: Explanatory Sequential Mixed Methods
methodologies.description: A two-phase mixed methods design where quantitative data is collected and analyzed first, followed by qualitative data collection to help explain or elaborate on quantitative results. Qualitative phase builds on quantitative findings. Priority often given to quantitative strand. Useful when quantitative results need deeper understanding or explanation.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Quantitative first, then qualitative to explain results

vs_similar_methods.difference: Explanatory is quant→qual; exploratory is qual→quant
Explanatory is sequential; convergent is concurrent
Explanatory is planned; post-hoc may be reactive

methodologies.name: Exploratory Sequential Mixed Methods
methodologies.description: A two-phase mixed methods design where qualitative data is collected first to explore a phenomenon, followed by quantitative data collection to test or generalize qualitative findings. Qualitative phase informs quantitative instrument development or hypotheses. Priority often given to qualitative strand. Useful for developing measures or testing emergent theory.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Qualitative first to explore, then quantitative to test/generalize

vs_similar_methods.difference: Exploratory is qual→quant; explanatory is quant→qual
Exploratory is sequential; convergent is concurrent
Exploratory is full design; scale development is specific application

methodologies.name: Embedded/Nested Mixed Methods
methodologies.description: A mixed methods design where one form of data (usually qualitative) is embedded within a larger design (usually quantitative) to address a secondary question. The embedded component provides supplementary information. Common in intervention trials where qualitative process evaluation is embedded. Unequal priority with primary strand dominant.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: One data type nested within a larger design dominated by the other type

vs_similar_methods.difference: Embedded has unequal priority; convergent is equal
Embedded is simultaneous nesting; explanatory is sequential
Embedded is design type; process evaluation is specific application

methodologies.name: Transformative Mixed Methods
methodologies.description: A mixed methods design driven by a social justice, advocacy, or participatory theoretical lens. The transformative framework shapes all decisions about design, data collection, and interpretation. Aims to address power imbalances, give voice to marginalized groups, and promote social change. Less about specific timing and more about underlying paradigm.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Social justice or advocacy lens shapes all aspects of mixed methods design

vs_similar_methods.difference: Transformative is paradigm-driven; others are procedure-driven
Transformative may be participatory but broader concept
Transformative emphasizes theoretical lens; community-based emphasizes setting

methodologies.name: Multiphase Mixed Methods
methodologies.description: A complex mixed methods design involving multiple phases of quantitative and qualitative research over extended time, often aligned with program development or evaluation. Each phase builds on previous phases. Used for large-scale initiatives like intervention development, implementation, and evaluation. Requires substantial resources and long-term commitment.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Multiple sequential phases of mixed methods research over extended timeframe

vs_similar_methods.difference: Multiphase has many phases; sequential typically two
Multiphase combines methods; longitudinal may be single method
Multiphase is design type; program evaluation is application

methodologies.name: Standards-Based Clinical Audit
methodologies.description: A systematic review of clinical practice against explicit evidence-based standards or guidelines. Identifies gaps between actual and desired practice. The foundation of clinical audit - compares 'what we do' against 'what we should do'. Highly trainee-feasible and often required for UK foundation/specialty training. UK mandatory for ARCP sign-off at various levels.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Compares current practice against explicit pre-defined standards

vs_similar_methods.difference: Audit compares to standards; research generates new knowledge
Audit uses explicit standards; service evaluation describes current practice
Audit measures against standard; QI implements improvement

methodologies.name: Prescribing Audit
methodologies.description: A specialized clinical audit focused on medication prescribing practices. Compares prescribing against guidelines, formularies, or best practice standards. Common in psychiatry (RANZCP core requirement), antimicrobial stewardship, and chronic disease management. Reviews appropriateness, safety, and cost-effectiveness of prescribing patterns.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Focuses specifically on medication prescribing against best practice standards

vs_similar_methods.difference: Prescribing audit is medication-specific; general covers any practice
Prescribing audit compares to standards; DUS may be descriptive
Prescribing audit is systematic; safety review may be reactive

methodologies.name: Process Audit
methodologies.description: An audit focusing on the processes of care delivery - what is done to or for patients rather than structures or outcomes. Examines whether recommended care processes are being followed. Common targets include referral processes, care pathways, handover procedures, and diagnostic workflows.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Focuses on care delivery processes rather than structures or patient outcomes

vs_similar_methods.difference: Process examines what is done; outcome examines results
Process examines activities; structure examines resources
Process audit is against standards; workflow may be descriptive

methodologies.name: Outcome Audit
methodologies.description: An audit focusing on patient outcomes - the end results of healthcare. Measures outcomes against expected standards or benchmarks. May examine clinical outcomes (mortality, complications), patient-reported outcomes, or functional outcomes. More challenging than process audit due to case-mix adjustment needs.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Focuses on patient results and outcomes against benchmarks

vs_similar_methods.difference: Outcome examines results; process examines activities
Audit compares to standards; research tests hypotheses
Audit is local improvement focus; registry may be research

methodologies.name: Morbidity & Mortality (M&M) Audit
methodologies.description: A systematic review of adverse outcomes, complications, and deaths to identify system failures and learning opportunities. A staple of surgical and ICU training. ACGME-mandated in US surgical programs. Focuses on systems rather than individual blame. Requires psychological safety for honest discussion. Educational and quality improvement purposes.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Systematic review of adverse outcomes for system learning and improvement

vs_similar_methods.difference: M&M is regular review; RCA is deep single-event analysis
M&M focuses on adverse outcomes; case conference may be educational
M&M is structured review; incident reporting is notification

methodologies.name: Documentation Audit
methodologies.description: An audit assessing the quality and completeness of medical documentation against standards. Reviews clinical notes, consent forms, discharge summaries, or other records. Important for medico-legal compliance, communication, and care continuity. Common trainee audit topic due to accessibility.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Assesses written/electronic documentation quality against standards

vs_similar_methods.difference: Documentation audit assesses quality; chart review extracts clinical data
Documentation focuses on clinical content; coding on billing codes
Documentation is records-specific; compliance is broader

methodologies.name: Adverse Event Audit
methodologies.description: Systematic review of adverse events, incidents, or patient safety events against expected rates or to identify patterns. May be triggered by incident reports or proactive surveillance. Aims to identify system vulnerabilities and prevent recurrence. Distinct from research in its improvement focus.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Systematic review of safety events for pattern identification and prevention

vs_similar_methods.difference: Adverse event audit is broader; M&M focuses on mortality/morbidity
Adverse event audit reviews multiple events; RCA is single event
Audit analyzes reports; reporting is data collection

methodologies.name: Referral Audit
methodologies.description: An audit examining the appropriateness, quality, and timeliness of referrals between services. Assesses whether referrals meet criteria, contain necessary information, and result in appropriate outcomes. Important for service interface quality and resource utilization.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Examines quality and appropriateness of referrals between services

vs_similar_methods.difference: Referral audit is referral-specific; process audit is broader
Referral audit assesses appropriateness; access studies waiting times
Referral audit focuses on referral point; pathway is entire journey

methodologies.name: Waiting Time/Access Audit
methodologies.description: An audit examining timeliness of care - waiting times from referral to appointment, diagnosis to treatment, or between care stages. Compares against targets (e.g., NHS 18-week pathway, cancer waiting times). Important for service delivery and patient experience.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Examines timeliness of care against wait time targets or standards

vs_similar_methods.difference: Waiting time focuses on timing; process on activities
Waiting time audit is against standards; flow is descriptive
Audit compares to targets; research may explore factors

methodologies.name: Infection Control Audit
methodologies.description: An audit assessing compliance with infection prevention and control measures. Includes hand hygiene compliance, isolation precautions, catheter care, surgical site infection prevention, and environmental cleanliness. Mandatory component of healthcare accreditation. Often uses direct observation.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Assesses compliance with infection prevention protocols and standards

vs_similar_methods.difference: Audit measures compliance; surveillance tracks infection rates
Audit is improvement focus; research is knowledge generation
Infection control is specialized; general is broader

methodologies.name: Compliance Audit
methodologies.description: An audit assessing adherence to policies, protocols, guidelines, or regulatory requirements. Broader than specific clinical audits - may cover consent processes, mandatory training, professional standards, or organizational policies. Often required for accreditation or regulatory compliance.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Assesses adherence to policies, regulations, or organizational requirements

vs_similar_methods.difference: Compliance is policy-focused; clinical is care-focused
Compliance is broader; documentation is records-specific
Compliance measures adherence; quality may assess effectiveness

methodologies.name: Re-Audit (Full Cycle Audit)
methodologies.description: A repeat audit conducted after implementing changes following initial audit findings. Demonstrates whether improvement interventions were effective - 'closing the loop'. Essential component of audit cycle. RANZCP and UK training programs specifically require evidence of re-audit/full cycle completion. Higher value than single audits in portfolios.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Repeat audit after improvement intervention to demonstrate change

vs_similar_methods.difference: Re-audit measures improvement; initial identifies gaps
Re-audit is single repeat; PDSA is iterative improvement
Re-audit uses same standards; evaluation may be broader

methodologies.name: Service Evaluation
methodologies.description: A UK-specific category describing an evaluation of a service to see how well it is achieving its purpose. Unlike audit, does not compare against pre-defined standards - instead describes current service delivery. Does not require ethics approval in UK. Distinct from research which generates generalizable knowledge.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Describes how well a service is achieving its intended purpose without pre-defined standards

vs_similar_methods.difference: Service evaluation has no pre-defined standards; audit compares to standards
Service evaluation is local improvement; research generates generalizable knowledge
Service evaluation is descriptive; QI implements change

methodologies.name: PDSA Cycle
methodologies.description: Plan-Do-Study-Act - an iterative, four-stage improvement method from the IHI Model for Improvement. Tests changes on small scale before broader implementation. Emphasizes rapid cycles of learning. Multiple PDSA cycles refine interventions progressively. Uses run charts to track progress. Core methodology for quality improvement.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Iterative rapid-cycle testing of changes using four-stage Plan-Do-Study-Act

vs_similar_methods.difference: PDSA tests changes iteratively; audit measures against standards
PDSA is multiple small tests; pre-post is single comparison
PDSA is rapid iterative; Six Sigma is structured project

methodologies.name: Root Cause Analysis (RCA)
methodologies.description: A systematic investigation of a single adverse event to identify the root causes (system factors) that led to it. Uses structured tools like fishbone diagrams, 5 Whys, or timeline analysis. Joint Commission requires RCA for sentinel events in US. Focuses on system failures not individual blame. Produces action plan for prevention.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Deep systematic investigation of single serious event to identify root causes

vs_similar_methods.difference: RCA is deep single-event; M&M reviews multiple events
RCA is investigation; incident report is notification
RCA is reactive to events; FMEA is proactive risk assessment

recommended_sources: VA National Center for Patient Safety

methodologies.name: Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA)
methodologies.description: A proactive risk assessment method that systematically identifies potential failures in a process before they occur, assesses their severity and likelihood, and prioritizes prevention efforts. Calculates Risk Priority Number (RPN) for each failure mode. Originally from engineering/aerospace, adapted for healthcare. Prospective rather than reactive.
