differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Proactive prospective identification of potential failures before they cause harm

vs_similar_methods.difference: FMEA is proactive/prospective; RCA is reactive to events
FMEA has specific RPN methodology; risk assessment may vary
FMEA adds risk analysis; process mapping is descriptive

methodologies.name: Lean Methodology
methodologies.description: A systematic approach to eliminating waste and improving value in processes. Originated from Toyota Production System. Core concepts include value stream mapping, eliminating 7 wastes (muda), continuous flow, and pull systems. Focuses on what adds value from customer (patient) perspective. Widely applied in healthcare operations.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Systematic elimination of waste to maximize value using Toyota-derived principles

vs_similar_methods.difference: Lean focuses on waste/flow; Six Sigma on variation reduction
Lean is broader philosophy; PDSA is specific testing method
Lean has specific tools/philosophy; process improvement is generic

methodologies.name: Six Sigma
methodologies.description: A data-driven methodology for reducing variation and defects in processes. Uses statistical methods to achieve near-perfect quality (3.4 defects per million opportunities). DMAIC framework (Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve, Control) guides projects. Requires statistical expertise. Often combined with Lean (Lean Six Sigma). Belt certification system (Green Belt, Black Belt).

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Statistical approach to reducing process variation using DMAIC framework

vs_similar_methods.difference: Six Sigma reduces variation; Lean eliminates waste
Six Sigma is statistical/structured; PDSA is rapid testing
Six Sigma is project methodology; SPC is monitoring tool

methodologies.name: Significant Event Analysis (SEA)
methodologies.description: A UK-originated reflective learning method where a significant event (positive or negative) is analyzed systematically for learning and improvement. Common in UK general practice - required for GP training portfolios. Individual or team-based reflection. Includes events beyond patient safety to include significant learning moments.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Structured reflective analysis of significant events for learning and improvement

vs_similar_methods.difference: SEA is reflective learning; RCA is formal investigation
SEA is primary care focused; M&M is hospital-based
SEA has specific structure; reflection may be informal

methodologies.name: After Action Review (AAR)
methodologies.description: A structured team debrief conducted shortly after an event to capture learning while memory is fresh. Originated in US military. Asks four key questions: What was supposed to happen? What actually happened? Why the difference? What can we learn? Quick, non-punitive, action-oriented. Useful after simulations, resuscitations, or significant clinical events.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Immediate structured team debrief using four key questions for rapid learning

vs_similar_methods.difference: AAR is immediate/brief; M&M is scheduled/comprehensive
AAR is quick learning; RCA is formal investigation
AAR has specific four-question structure; debrief may be informal

methodologies.name: Case Report
methodologies.description: A detailed narrative description of a single unique or instructive clinical case. The most accessible publication type for trainees - 40-50% of medical students author case reports. Documents novel presentations, rare conditions, unexpected outcomes, or educational cases. Uses CARE guidelines for reporting. BMJ Case Reports is a popular venue with high acceptance rates.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Single detailed clinical case with educational or novel significance

vs_similar_methods.difference: Case report is single case; series is multiple
Case report is narrative description; chart review is data extraction
Case report is observational; N-of-1 is experimental

methodologies.name: Case Series
methodologies.description: A descriptive study reporting on a group of patients with similar diagnosis or treatment without a comparison group. Describes characteristics, treatments, and outcomes of consecutive or selected cases. More robust than single case reports but still descriptive. 8% of RANZCP scholarly projects are case series with 60% pass rate. Requires larger numbers than case reports.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Multiple cases described without control group - descriptive study of patient group

vs_similar_methods.difference: Series has multiple patients; report is single
Series has no comparison group; cohort compares exposed vs unexposed
Series has no controls; case-control has comparison group

methodologies.name: Image Case Report
methodologies.description: A case report format centered on striking or instructive clinical images - radiological, dermatological, pathological, or other visual findings. Brief text accompanies the image. Popular in specialties with strong visual components. Many journals have dedicated sections (Images in Clinical Medicine, Photo Quiz). Very accessible publication type.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Case report format with clinical image as central focus

vs_similar_methods.difference: Image case centers on visual; standard is narrative-focused
Image case is single case visual; series is multiple patients
Image case is publication; teaching file may be internal

methodologies.name: Video Case Report
methodologies.description: A case report format featuring video documentation of clinical findings, procedures, or techniques. Particularly valuable for surgical techniques, movement disorders, procedural skills, and dynamic clinical findings. Growing format with online-first publication. Requires appropriate equipment and consent for video recording.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Case report format with video as primary media documenting clinical findings or procedures

vs_similar_methods.difference: Video case uses video; standard is text/images
Video is dynamic; image is static
Video case is case-based; technical note describes method

methodologies.name: Curriculum Evaluation Study
methodologies.description: Research evaluating the design, implementation, or outcomes of an educational curriculum or program. Uses various methodologies (surveys, assessments, qualitative) to assess whether educational objectives are being met. Common for trainees in academic medicine (10-15% of trainee projects). Kirkpatrick's four-level model often frames outcomes: reaction, learning, behavior, results.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Evaluates educational program effectiveness - focused on curriculum/learning outcomes

vs_similar_methods.difference: Evaluation assesses existing program; intervention tests new approach
Curriculum evaluation specifically about education; survey may be any topic
Curriculum evaluation in education; QI in clinical care

methodologies.name: Educational Intervention Study
methodologies.description: A study testing the effectiveness of an educational intervention compared to control or alternative conditions. May use pre-post, controlled, or randomized designs. Aims to determine whether a teaching method, curriculum change, or educational tool improves learning outcomes. BEME guidelines for reporting.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Tests effect of educational intervention on learning outcomes

vs_similar_methods.difference: Intervention tests new approach; evaluation assesses existing
Educational intervention targets learning; clinical targets health
Educational intervention is broader; simulation is specific modality

methodologies.name: Assessment/Exam Validation Study
methodologies.description: Research examining the validity, reliability, and other psychometric properties of educational assessments. May develop new assessments or validate existing ones in new contexts. Uses frameworks like Messick's validity or Kane's argument-based validity. Contributes to assessment quality in medical education.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Examines psychometric properties of educational assessments

vs_similar_methods.difference: Assessment validation focuses on test; curriculum on program
Assessment is educational context; scale may be any domain
Validation is comprehensive; reliability is one component

methodologies.name: Faculty Development Evaluation
methodologies.description: Research evaluating programs designed to improve teaching skills, educational leadership, or scholarly abilities of faculty. Uses similar frameworks to curriculum evaluation but focused on teachers rather than learners. Growing area as institutions invest in teaching quality.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Evaluates programs designed to develop faculty educational abilities

vs_similar_methods.difference: Faculty development focuses on teachers; curriculum on students
Faculty development is formal program; workplace may be informal
Faculty development is educational focus; leadership may be clinical/administrative

methodologies.name: Simulation-Based Research
methodologies.description: Research conducted in simulation environments to study learning, assessment, or human factors. May use manikins, standardized patients, virtual reality, or task trainers. Can study skill acquisition, team performance, or compare training modalities. Growing rapidly with technology advances.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Uses simulation environments to study learning, assessment, or performance

vs_similar_methods.difference: Simulation is simulated setting; clinical is real patients
Simulation is specific modality; intervention may be any type
Simulation research is broader; assessment may be one component

methodologies.name: Competency-Based Assessment Research
methodologies.description: Research on competency-based medical education (CBME) frameworks, including Entrustable Professional Activities (EPAs), milestones, and competency assessment methods. Growing field as medical education shifts from time-based to competency-based progression.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Studies competency frameworks, EPAs, milestones, and competency-based progression

vs_similar_methods.difference: CBME focuses on competency frameworks; validation may be any assessment
CBME is assessment/progression focused; curriculum is broader
CBME research studies the system; workplace assessment is the tool

methodologies.name: Needs Assessment Study
methodologies.description: A systematic process to identify gaps between current and desired knowledge, skills, or attitudes to inform educational program development. Surveys stakeholders, reviews performance data, or uses other methods to determine learning needs. First step in curriculum development cycle.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Identifies educational gaps to inform program development

vs_similar_methods.difference: Needs assessment is pre-curriculum; evaluation is post
Needs assessment specifically identifies gaps; survey may be any topic
Needs assessment identifies gaps; competency measures achievement

methodologies.name: Program Evaluation Study
methodologies.description: Comprehensive evaluation of an educational program using established frameworks like RE-AIM (Reach, Effectiveness, Adoption, Implementation, Maintenance) or Kirkpatrick's levels. Broader than curriculum evaluation - includes implementation factors, sustainability, and program-level outcomes.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Comprehensive evaluation of educational program using established frameworks

vs_similar_methods.difference: Program evaluation is comprehensive; curriculum may focus on content
Program evaluation is educational focus; implementation is clinical
Program evaluation is educational; service is clinical service

methodologies.name: Delphi Study
methodologies.description: A structured consensus methodology using iterative rounds of anonymous questionnaires with controlled feedback to reach group agreement on topics where evidence is incomplete. Experts rate items, receive summarized group responses, and re-rate until consensus achieved. Common in medical education (40.8% of consensus studies) for curriculum development, competency definition, and guideline development.

included_subtypes.name: Real-time Delphi
included_subtypes.description: Continuous access to evolving group view

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Anonymous iterative expert rating with controlled feedback until consensus

vs_similar_methods.difference: Delphi is anonymous remote; NGT is face-to-face
Delphi is more iterative; RAND uses literature first
Delphi is structured anonymous; committee may have dominance

methodologies.name: Modified Delphi Study
methodologies.description: A variation of the Delphi method that typically starts with a structured questionnaire derived from literature review rather than open-ended first round. More common than classical Delphi in medical education (35.4% of consensus studies). Faster and more focused but may miss novel ideas.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Delphi starting with pre-defined items rather than open exploration

vs_similar_methods.difference: Modified is structured from start; classical has open first round
Modified Delphi is iterative; RAND has face-to-face component
Modified Delphi has iteration and feedback; survey is single round

methodologies.name: Nominal Group Technique (NGT)
methodologies.description: A structured face-to-face group process for generating ideas and reaching consensus. Involves silent individual idea generation, round-robin sharing, group discussion, and voting/ranking. 8.9% of medical education consensus studies. More interactive than Delphi but susceptible to group dynamics.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Structured face-to-face group process with individual work then group discussion

vs_similar_methods.difference: NGT is face-to-face; Delphi is remote anonymous
NGT has structured voting; focus group is discussion-based
NGT has silent generation first; brainstorming is open verbal

methodologies.name: RAND/UCLA Appropriateness Method
methodologies.description: A structured method combining systematic literature review with expert panel judgment to develop appropriateness criteria for clinical procedures or interventions. Two-round process: individual rating followed by face-to-face meeting with discussion and re-rating. Gold standard for clinical appropriateness guidelines.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Combines literature synthesis with structured expert judgment for appropriateness criteria

vs_similar_methods.difference: RAND has face-to-face component; Delphi is fully remote
RAND produces appropriateness ratings; guidelines are recommendations
RAND includes literature review; NGT may not

methodologies.name: Consensus Conference
methodologies.description: A formal meeting of experts convened to reach agreement on specific questions through structured deliberation. May result in consensus statements, guidelines, or recommendations. Often sponsored by professional organizations. Requires careful planning and facilitation.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Formal structured expert meeting to reach agreement through deliberation

vs_similar_methods.difference: Consensus conference is face-to-face meeting; Delphi is remote
Consensus conference produces consensus; guideline has formal process
Consensus conference is time-limited event; committee may be ongoing

methodologies.name: Scale Development/Validation Study
methodologies.description: Research developing new measurement instruments (questionnaires, scales) or validating existing ones in new populations. Follows established steps: item generation, content validity, pilot testing, factor analysis, reliability, criterion validity. Essential for creating tools used in clinical and research settings.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Creates or validates measurement instruments through systematic psychometric evaluation

vs_similar_methods.difference: Scale development creates/validates tool; survey uses existing tools
Scale development is comprehensive; reliability is one component
Scale development is measurement focus; diagnostic is test performance

methodologies.name: Diagnostic Accuracy Study
methodologies.description: Research evaluating how well a diagnostic test correctly identifies patients with and without a target condition compared to a reference standard. Reports sensitivity, specificity, predictive values, and likelihood ratios. STARD guidelines for reporting. Essential for evidence-based test interpretation.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Evaluates test performance against reference standard - sensitivity, specificity, predictive values

vs_similar_methods.difference: Diagnostic accuracy compares to reference; validation assesses properties
Diagnostic accuracy is methodology; screening is application
Diagnostic accuracy is single test; prediction rule combines variables

methodologies.name: Reliability Study
methodologies.description: Research assessing the consistency and reproducibility of measurements. Includes test-retest reliability (stability over time), inter-rater reliability (agreement between raters), and internal consistency (coherence of items). Essential component of measurement quality assessment.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Assesses consistency and reproducibility of measurements

vs_similar_methods.difference: Reliability is consistency; validity is accuracy
Reliability uses correlation; agreement uses absolute difference
Reliability study is research; QC is ongoing monitoring

methodologies.name: Measurement Invariance Study
methodologies.description: Research testing whether a measurement instrument functions equivalently across different groups (gender, age, culture) or time points. Uses multi-group confirmatory factor analysis to test configural, metric, and scalar invariance. Essential for fair comparisons across groups.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Tests whether measurement functions equivalently across groups or time

vs_similar_methods.difference: Invariance tests equivalence; validation assesses properties
Invariance tests statistically; adaptation is translation process
Invariance is CFA-based; DIF is item-level IRT

methodologies.name: Item Response Theory (IRT) Study
methodologies.description: Modern psychometric approach analyzing item-level properties using mathematical models. Provides item difficulty, discrimination, and information functions. Enables computerized adaptive testing, test equating, and more nuanced measurement. More sophisticated than classical test theory.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Analyzes item-level properties using mathematical models rather than total scores

vs_similar_methods.difference: IRT is item-focused; CTT is test-focused
IRT models item responses; FA identifies dimensions
IRT provides framework; DIF is specific application

methodologies.name: Clinical Prediction Rule Development
methodologies.description: Research developing statistical models that combine clinical variables to predict diagnosis, prognosis, or treatment response. Uses regression or machine learning to identify and weight predictors. TRIPOD guidelines for reporting. Three phases: derivation, validation, impact assessment.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Develops statistical model combining multiple predictors for clinical decision support

vs_similar_methods.difference: Prediction rule combines variables; diagnostic accuracy tests one test
Prediction rule is for individual prediction; risk factor is association
Clinical prediction rule is interpretable; ML may be black box

methodologies.name: Cost-Effectiveness Analysis (CEA)
methodologies.description: An economic evaluation comparing costs and health outcomes (usually natural units like life-years gained, cases prevented) of alternative interventions. Calculates incremental cost-effectiveness ratio (ICER). Most common health economic method. Essential for HTA submissions. CHEERS 2022 reporting standard.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Compares costs to health outcomes in natural units to calculate cost per outcome gained

vs_similar_methods.difference: CEA uses natural units; CUA uses QALYs
CEA measures health outcomes; CBA monetizes all outcomes
CEA is efficiency; BIA is affordability

methodologies.name: Cost-Utility Analysis (CUA)
methodologies.description: A form of cost-effectiveness analysis that measures health outcomes in quality-adjusted life years (QALYs) or disability-adjusted life years (DALYs). Preferred by NICE, PBAC, and other HTA bodies because QALYs allow comparison across disease areas. Results expressed as cost per QALY gained.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Uses QALYs or DALYs as outcome measure enabling cross-disease comparison

vs_similar_methods.difference: CUA uses QALYs; CEA uses natural units
CUA uses QALYs; CBA monetizes outcomes
CUA is full economic evaluation; utility elicitation measures preferences

methodologies.name: Cost-Benefit Analysis (CBA)
methodologies.description: An economic evaluation where all outcomes (health and non-health) are valued in monetary terms. Allows direct comparison of costs and benefits and calculation of net benefit. Less common in healthcare than CEA/CUA because monetizing health is controversial. More common in public policy and environmental economics.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Monetizes all outcomes including health to calculate net monetary benefit

vs_similar_methods.difference: CBA monetizes health; CEA uses natural units
CBA uses monetary outcomes; CUA uses QALYs
CBA is full evaluation; WTP measures willingness to pay only

methodologies.name: Budget Impact Analysis (BIA)
methodologies.description: An analysis estimating the financial impact of adopting a new intervention on the healthcare budget over a specified time horizon (typically 1-5 years). Focuses on affordability rather than efficiency. Required by many HTA bodies alongside cost-effectiveness. ISPOR BIA guidelines.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Estimates total budget impact of adoption rather than efficiency per patient

vs_similar_methods.difference: BIA is affordability; CEA is efficiency
BIA is population budget; cost analysis may be per-patient
BIA is HTA-focused; financial may be organizational

methodologies.name: Decision-Analytic Modeling
methodologies.description: The use of mathematical models to synthesize evidence and compare long-term costs and outcomes of alternative strategies. Includes decision trees, Markov models, discrete event simulation, and microsimulation. Core methodology for model-based economic evaluations extending beyond trial data.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Uses mathematical structures to extrapolate costs and outcomes beyond observed data

vs_similar_methods.difference: Modeling extrapolates; trial-based uses observed data
Modeling synthesizes for decisions; meta-analysis pools effects
Decision model is for decision comparison; statistical may be descriptive

methodologies.name: Cost-Minimization Analysis
methodologies.description: An economic evaluation comparing costs of interventions when outcomes are assumed to be equivalent. The least expensive option is preferred. Requires strong evidence that outcomes are truly equivalent (often from equivalence trial). Simpler than other economic evaluations but limited applicability.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Compares costs only when outcomes are assumed or proven equivalent

vs_similar_methods.difference: CMA assumes equal outcomes; CEA measures differential outcomes
CMA requires equivalence evidence; cost analysis may not
CMA is per-patient; BIA is population-level

methodologies.name: Willingness-to-Pay Study
methodologies.description: Research eliciting monetary valuations for health interventions or outcomes using stated preference methods. Includes contingent valuation and discrete choice experiments. Used to understand patient/societal preferences and inform cost-benefit analyses. Methodologically complex with hypothetical bias concerns.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Elicits monetary valuations for health through stated preference methods

vs_similar_methods.difference: WTP provides values for CBA; CBA is full evaluation
WTP is monetary; utility is preference-based score
WTP may be direct question; DCE uses choice sets

methodologies.name: Implementation Study
methodologies.description: Research examining the process and outcomes of implementing evidence-based practices, interventions, or policies in real-world settings. Uses implementation science frameworks (CFIR, RE-AIM, etc.) to understand barriers, facilitators, and strategies. Distinct from effectiveness research - focuses on the 'how' of implementation rather than 'does it work'.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Studies the process of implementing evidence-based practices using implementation frameworks

vs_similar_methods.difference: Implementation studies 'how' to implement; effectiveness studies 'does it work'
Implementation is research-focused; QI is local improvement
Implementation uses IS frameworks; process may be atheoretical

methodologies.name: Hybrid Effectiveness-Implementation Trial
methodologies.description: A study design that simultaneously tests clinical effectiveness and implementation strategies. Three types: Type 1 (primarily effectiveness, secondary implementation); Type 2 (equal focus); Type 3 (primarily implementation, secondary effectiveness). Accelerates translation by combining research phases.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Simultaneously tests clinical effectiveness and implementation strategies

vs_similar_methods.difference: Hybrid includes implementation; effectiveness does not
Hybrid tests effectiveness; implementation assumes it
Hybrid has dual outcomes; pragmatic may not study implementation

methodologies.name: Knowledge Translation Study
methodologies.description: Research on processes that move knowledge from research to practice. Broader term common in Canada encompassing knowledge creation, synthesis, exchange, and application. Uses KT frameworks like the Knowledge-to-Action cycle. Emphasizes stakeholder engagement and context adaptation.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Studies movement of knowledge from research to practice using KT frameworks

vs_similar_methods.difference: KT is broader term including creation; IS focuses on uptake
KT includes knowledge exchange; dissemination is one-way
KT is application focused; translational may include T1-T4

methodologies.name: Feasibility/Pilot Implementation Study
methodologies.description: A preliminary study testing the feasibility of implementing an intervention or implementation strategy before a larger trial. Assesses acceptability, demand, practicality, and limited efficacy. Distinct from clinical pilot studies by focus on implementation processes. Essential precursor to full implementation trials.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Tests feasibility of implementation processes before larger study

vs_similar_methods.difference: Implementation pilot focuses on implementation; clinical on intervention
Pilot is preliminary; full is definitive
Pilot is before main study; formative during

methodologies.name: Fidelity Study
methodologies.description: Research examining the degree to which an intervention is delivered as intended. Assesses adherence, dose, quality, and responsiveness. Essential for understanding whether implementation reflects the evidence-based practice. May be standalone or embedded within larger studies.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Measures the degree to which intervention is delivered as intended

vs_similar_methods.difference: Fidelity focuses on delivery accuracy; process is broader
Fidelity is research; QA is operational
Fidelity measures adherence; adaptation studies modifications

methodologies.name: Process Evaluation
methodologies.description: A study examining implementation processes alongside outcome evaluation, typically in trials or programs. MRC guidance recommends examining implementation (fidelity, dose, adaptations), mechanisms of impact, and context. Often mixed methods. Essential for understanding how and why interventions work.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Examines implementation, mechanisms, and context alongside outcomes

vs_similar_methods.difference: Process examines 'how'; outcome examines 'what'
Process is broader; fidelity is one component
Process may be atheoretical; implementation uses IS frameworks

methodologies.name: Realist Evaluation
methodologies.description: An evaluation approach based on realist philosophy that seeks to understand 'what works, for whom, in what circumstances, and why'. Develops Context-Mechanism-Outcome (CMO) configurations. Particularly suited for complex interventions. Theory-driven approach testing program theories.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Uses CMO configurations to understand what works, for whom, and in what circumstances

vs_similar_methods.difference: Realist is theory-driven CMO; process may be atheoretical
Realist explains mechanisms; RCT tests effects
Realist has specific CMO framework; qualitative varies

methodologies.name: Adaptation Study
methodologies.description: Research examining how evidence-based interventions are modified for new contexts while maintaining core components. Studies intentional adaptations (planned modifications) and fidelity-consistent vs. inconsistent changes. Uses frameworks like FRAME to document adaptations systematically.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Studies how interventions are modified for new contexts while maintaining effectiveness

vs_similar_methods.difference: Adaptation studies changes; fidelity measures adherence
Adaptation modifies intervention; validation tests measure
Adaptation focuses on modifications; pilot on feasibility

methodologies.name: Sustainability Study
methodologies.description: Research examining the long-term maintenance of implemented interventions after initial implementation support ends. Studies factors predicting sustainment, de-implementation of low-value practices, and spread of innovations. Critical for ensuring implementation investments have lasting impact.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Studies long-term maintenance of implemented interventions beyond initial support

vs_similar_methods.difference: Sustainability is post-implementation; implementation is during
Sustainability focuses on practice maintenance; follow-up on patient outcomes
Sustainability is maintenance; scale-up is expansion

methodologies.name: Scale-Up/Spread Study
methodologies.description: Research examining the expansion of successfully implemented interventions to new sites, populations, or systems. Studies strategies for spread, barriers at scale, and adaptation during expansion. WHO ExpandNet provides framework. Critical for maximizing population health impact of effective interventions.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Studies expansion of successful implementations to new sites, populations, or systems

vs_similar_methods.difference: Scale-up is expansion; implementation may be single site
Scale-up is expansion; sustainability is maintenance
Scale-up is deliberate; diffusion may be natural

methodologies.name: In Vitro Study
methodologies.description: Laboratory research conducted outside living organisms, typically using cells, tissues, or biological molecules in controlled environments (test tubes, petri dishes, cell cultures). Foundation of drug discovery and mechanism understanding. Highly controlled but limited translation to in vivo and human contexts.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Laboratory research outside living organisms using cells, tissues, or molecules

vs_similar_methods.difference: In vitro is outside organism; in vivo is in living organism
In vitro is lab-based; clinical is in humans
In vitro uses cultured cells; ex vivo uses freshly isolated tissue

methodologies.name: Animal Study
methodologies.description: Research conducted in non-human animals to understand disease mechanisms, test interventions, or model human conditions. Uses various species depending on research question. Requires ethical approval and adherence to 3Rs (Replace, Reduce, Refine). ARRIVE 2.0 guidelines for reporting. Essential translational step but species differences limit generalizability.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Research in living animals to model human biology and disease

vs_similar_methods.difference: Animal is in living organism; in vitro is lab bench
Animal is non-human; clinical is human
Experimental involves intervention; observational does not

methodologies.name: Ex Vivo Study
methodologies.description: Research using living tissues or organs removed from an organism and maintained in artificial conditions for a limited time. Preserves tissue architecture and cell-cell interactions better than cell culture. Uses surgical specimens, biopsies, or cadaveric tissue. Bridge between in vitro and in vivo.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Uses freshly isolated living tissues maintained outside the body

vs_similar_methods.difference: Ex vivo uses intact tissue; in vitro uses cultured cells
Ex vivo is removed from organism; in vivo is within organism
Ex vivo is primary tissue; organoid is grown from stem cells

methodologies.name: Computational/In Silico Study
methodologies.description: Research using computer modeling and simulation to study biological systems, predict drug behavior, or analyze complex datasets. Includes molecular modeling, pharmacokinetic simulation, and machine learning for drug discovery. Increasingly important in personalized medicine and drug development.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Uses computer modeling and simulation rather than wet lab or clinical experiments

vs_similar_methods.difference: In silico is computational; in vitro is bench experiments
In silico is simulation; clinical is human testing
In silico models biology; statistics analyzes data

recommended_sources: Journal of Chemical Information and Modeling

methodologies.name: Translational Study (T1)
methodologies.description: Research bridging basic science discoveries to first-in-human applications (bench to bedside). T1 translation focuses on moving from preclinical to Phase I/II trials. Includes biomarker development, proof-of-concept studies, and early clinical translation. Part of the T1-T4 translational spectrum.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Bridges basic science to first human applications - bench to bedside

vs_similar_methods.difference: T1 aims at human application; basic is knowledge generation
T1 enables trials; clinical tests in humans
T1 is bench to bedside; T2 is bedside to practice

methodologies.name: Biomarker Discovery/Validation Study
methodologies.description: Research identifying and validating biological markers for disease diagnosis, prognosis, or treatment selection. Discovery phase identifies candidate markers; validation phase tests in independent cohorts. Uses omics technologies (genomics, proteomics, metabolomics). Critical for precision medicine.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Identifies and validates biological markers for clinical use

vs_similar_methods.difference: Biomarker study may include discovery; diagnostic tests established markers
Biomarker has clinical application goal; genomics may be descriptive
Biomarker uses biological markers; prediction may use clinical variables

methodologies.name: Pharmacokinetic/Pharmacodynamic Study
methodologies.description: Research characterizing drug absorption, distribution, metabolism, and excretion (PK) and the relationship between drug concentration and effect (PD). May be preclinical, clinical, or population-based. Essential for drug development, dosing optimization, and understanding variability.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Characterizes drug absorption, distribution, metabolism, excretion, and effects

vs_similar_methods.difference: PK/PD focuses on drug behavior; trial on clinical outcomes
PK/PD characterizes single drug; interaction studies multiple
PK/PD is mechanistic; dose-finding is optimal dose selection

methodologies.name: Toxicology Study
methodologies.description: Research examining the adverse effects of substances on living organisms. In drug development, preclinical toxicology studies are regulatory requirements before human testing. Includes acute, chronic, reproductive, and genetic toxicology. GLP (Good Laboratory Practice) compliance required for regulatory submissions.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Examines adverse effects and safety profile of substances

vs_similar_methods.difference: Toxicology studies harm; efficacy studies benefit
Toxicology is preclinical; Phase I is human safety
Toxicology is preclinical; post-marketing is after approval

methodologies.name: Conference Oral Presentation
methodologies.description: A formal verbal presentation of research findings at a professional conference. Typically 10-20 minutes with slides. Competitive selection through abstract review. Important for dissemination and career development. Often precedes publication. Counted in academic portfolios.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Verbal presentation of research to conference audience with slides

vs_similar_methods.difference: Oral is verbal to room; poster is individual discussion
Presentation is preliminary; publication is final
Oral presentation is competitive; invited is by request

methodologies.name: Poster Presentation
methodologies.description: A visual display of research findings on a large poster at a professional conference. Allows one-on-one discussion with interested attendees. More common acceptance than oral presentations. Standard format varies but typically A0/48x36 inches. Important entry point for trainees.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Visual research display enabling individual attendee discussion

vs_similar_methods.difference: Poster is visual display; oral is verbal to room
Poster includes visual; abstract is text only
Poster is research communication; infographic is public communication

methodologies.name: Grand Rounds Presentation
methodologies.description: A formal educational presentation to a hospital or department audience, typically weekly. May present clinical cases, research findings, or educational topics. Traditional format in medical education. Not peer-reviewed like conference presentations but important for local dissemination and teaching.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Formal departmental/hospital presentation for education and dissemination

vs_similar_methods.difference: Grand rounds is local; conference is external
Grand rounds is formal whole-department; teaching may be small group
Grand rounds is formal presentation; case conference is discussion

methodologies.name: Research Seminar
methodologies.description: A presentation of research in progress or completed work to an academic audience for discussion and feedback. May be departmental, institutional, or inter-institutional. Often more detailed than conference presentations. Important for refining work before publication.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Academic presentation of research for detailed discussion and feedback

vs_similar_methods.difference: Seminar allows more discussion; conference is time-limited
Seminar is research-focused; grand rounds may be clinical
Seminar is informal feedback; defense is formal assessment

methodologies.name: Thesis/Dissertation Defense
methodologies.description: A formal oral examination where a candidate presents and defends their thesis or dissertation research to an examining committee. Required for research higher degrees (MD, PhD). High-stakes examination with pass/fail/revisions outcomes. Culmination of extended research training.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Formal examination of thesis/dissertation by expert committee

vs_similar_methods.difference: Defense is formal examination; seminar is informal feedback
Defense is degree requirement; conference is dissemination
Defense is oral examination; publication is written peer review

methodologies.name: Invited Lecture/Keynote
methodologies.description: A presentation requested by conference organizers or institutions based on expertise or achievement. Higher prestige than submitted presentations. Keynotes open major conferences; named lectures honor contributors. Recognition of standing in field.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Presentation by invitation based on expertise or achievement

vs_similar_methods.difference: Invited is by request; submitted is competitive
Invited is recognition-based; grand rounds is regular schedule
Invited recognizes expertise; teaching is curriculum delivery

methodologies.name: Commentary/Editorial
methodologies.description: A short invited or submitted piece providing opinion, perspective, or interpretation on a topic, often responding to a published article. Editorials are typically invited by editors; commentaries may be submitted. Counts as publication but not original research. Platform for expert perspective.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Short opinion or perspective piece rather than original research

vs_similar_methods.difference: Commentary is opinion; original is new research
Commentary is invited/longer; letter is brief response
Commentary is opinion; review synthesizes evidence

methodologies.name: Letter to the Editor
methodologies.description: A brief correspondence responding to a published article, raising a point, or presenting very brief findings. Usually <400 words. Quick publication turnaround. Accessible entry point for publication. May include original observations but limited scope.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Brief correspondence format - response or very brief findings

vs_similar_methods.difference: Letter is shorter; commentary is longer invited piece
Letter is briefer; case report is detailed narrative
Letter is brief; original is full research paper

methodologies.name: Book Chapter
methodologies.description: A contributed chapter to an edited academic book or textbook. Usually by invitation from book editors. May be review/synthesis, educational, or original contribution. Counts as publication but different from journal articles. Important for textbooks in specialty.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Contributed chapter to edited book rather than journal article

vs_similar_methods.difference: Chapter is in book; review is in journal
Chapter may be synthesis; article is original research
Chapter is contributed; monograph is single-author book

methodologies.name: Technical Report
methodologies.description: A detailed report of research findings, typically for a funding body, government agency, or institution. Not peer-reviewed like journal articles but may be substantial. Important for policy-relevant research. Gray literature status.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Detailed research report for specific audience - not peer-reviewed journal

vs_similar_methods.difference: Report is for funder/agency; article is peer-reviewed
Report is project-specific; thesis is degree requirement
Technical report is detailed; white paper may be advocacy

methodologies.name: Clinical Practice Guideline
methodologies.description: Systematically developed statements to assist practitioner and patient decisions about appropriate healthcare. Based on systematic evidence review. Developed by professional organizations or health agencies. AGREE II for quality assessment. High-impact scholarly output.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Systematically developed recommendations for clinical practice based on evidence

vs_similar_methods.difference: Guideline makes recommendations; review synthesizes evidence
Guideline is evidence-based; consensus may be expert opinion
Guideline is recommendations; protocol is specific procedures

methodologies.name: Patent/Intellectual Property
methodologies.description: Legal protection for novel inventions, devices, or processes arising from research. Increasingly valued in academic settings as indicator of translational potential. Requires novelty, non-obviousness, and utility. Complex process involving patent attorneys and technology transfer offices.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Legal protection for novel inventions rather than publication of findings

vs_similar_methods.difference: Patent protects IP; publication shares findings
Patent is legal protection; report is documentation
Patent protects invention; trademark protects brand

methodologies.name: Dataset Publication
methodologies.description: Formal publication of a research dataset in a data repository with persistent identifier (DOI). Enables data sharing, reuse, and reproducibility. Increasingly required by funders and journals. Data papers describe the dataset. Growing form of scholarly output.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Formal publication of dataset rather than analysis/findings

vs_similar_methods.difference: Dataset is raw data; article is analysis
Dataset publication is standalone; supplementary is with article
Dataset is specific project; database may be ongoing collection

methodologies.name: Software/Code Publication
methodologies.description: Formal release of research software, analysis code, or computational tools. May be through repositories (GitHub, Zenodo) with DOI, or software journals. Essential for reproducibility and reuse. Increasingly recognized as citable scholarly output.

differentiating_characteristics.primary_distinguisher: Formal release of software/code as scholarly output

vs_similar_methods.difference: Software is code; methods is description
Software is code; dataset is data
Software publication is standalone; supplementary is with article
